entities.name: vyavahāra
entities.cite: Vyavahara, the working practice of grammar and speech

entities.name: darśana
entities.cite: on either darsana: one school takes the parts as mukhya, primary, and constructs wholes; the other takes the whole as primary and extracts parts

entities.name: avayava
entities.cite: one school takes the parts as mukhya, primary

entities.name: avayavī
entities.cite: the other takes the whole as primary

entities.name: mukhya
entities.cite: what is primary for some is for others the reverse

axioms.name: anugamyate
axioms.cite: Vyavahara, the working practice of grammar and speech, anugamyate, proceeds, on either darsana

axioms.name: mukhya
axioms.cite: one school takes the parts as mukhya, primary, and constructs wholes

axioms.name: mukhya
axioms.cite: the other takes the whole as primary and extracts parts

axioms.name: viparyaya
axioms.cite: one school takes the parts as mukhya, primary, and constructs wholes; the other takes the whole as primary and extracts parts

denials.name: nirniyate
denials.cite: the choice between part-primacy and whole-primacy will not be settled by grammatical data, which both fit

claims.name: anugamyate
claims.cite: Practice is carried on by resort to differing views

rejected_readings.label: exclusive_part_primacy
rejected_readings.rendering: The parts are the only primary elements in grammatical analysis; the whole is always secondary and derived from parts alone.
rejected_readings.why: Asserts exclusive part-primacy (a stronger claim than the axioms allow), wrongly denying that the whole can also be mukhya. The commentary explicitly states both schools are valid: one takes parts as mukhya, the other takes wholes as mukhya.

rejected_readings.label: mutual_refutation
rejected_readings.rendering: The two schools of thought refute each other, making practice impossible since the views directly contradict.
rejected_readings.why: Wrongly asserts contradiction between the darsanas, contradicting the axiom that vyavahara anugamyate darsana (practice proceeds on either darsana equally). The commentary shows both can run the entire practice; viparyaya is a reversal of priority, not mutual refutation.

notes: The contract models two rival darsanas: one takes parts (avayava) as mukhya, the other the whole (avayavi). Both are viable for running grammatical practice (vyavahara). The viparyaya relation captures the reversal of priority between them. The denial registers that this philosophical difference cannot be settled by grammatical data alone—practice underdetermines metaphysics.
